title: Claude Channels Just Dropped, And It Kills OpenClaw (Again)
summary: Anthropic released Claude Channels, a new feature that encapsulates functionality previously available in OpenClaw. The feature allows users to set up integrated workflows across Discord, Telegram, and other platforms within Claude Code, demonstrated with setup guidance across multiple communication channels.
writeup: Claude Code's new channels feature routes incoming messages from Telegram and Discord into a running local Claude Code session, letting any configured skill or tool execute in response to a text message the same way it would from a desktop prompt. The demo covers two practical tasks: repurposing a YouTube thumbnail via a local image skill and scraping 98 leads into a CSV via an Apify integration, both triggered from a phone and delivered back over the messaging app. Security is handled through a per-plugin sender allow list that silently drops any message from an ID not pre-approved, preserving the existing Claude Code permission model. The author frames channels as Anthropic's direct answer to third-party tools like Open Claude that had already built this kind of messaging-app-to-agent bridge.
key_points: Claude Code channels push events into a live local session from Telegram or Discord, meaning the agent runs on the user's own machine with full access to locally configured skills and tools, not on a remote server.

Each approved channel plugin enforces a sender allow list: only pre-registered Telegram or Discord user IDs can trigger the agent, and all other messages are silently dropped, preserving the same security posture Claude Code had before channels existed.

The Telegram integration requires creating a bot via BotFather to obtain a token, installing the plugin with '/plugin install telegram@claude-plugins-official' at user scope, then running '/telegram:configure' with that token inside Claude Code.

A lead-scraping task triggered from Discord returned 98 phone-number-bearing contacts as a CSV delivered directly to the phone within seconds, illustrating that any existing Apify or similar skill works without reconfiguration.
The session also gets a local conversation-history update with a plain-language description of what the agent did, providing a lightweight audit trail alongside whatever the agent actually produced.
The author argues channels functionally replicate what Open Claude and similar third-party bots already offered, making those tools less differentiated now that the capability ships first-party.
